title: The People vs. AI: Behind the Cover
content:
In an era when artificial intelligence is swiftly being embedded into the fabric of daily work, a prominent magazine commissioned a unique photographic essay. Photographer Benedict Evans was sent across the United States to capture portraits of everyday citizens actively taking a stand against AI. At every stop, he meticulously arranged his mobile studio, measuring the exact distance between the backdrop, chair, and lighting. This rigorous consistency was designed to lend the entire portrait series a quiet visual cohesion and an uncompromising salute to human authorship. The ambitious project required a punishing travel schedule, taking the solo photographer to ten different cities in just fourteen days.

Evans, who is deeply skeptical of artificial intelligence himself, found the nationwide journey to be both challenging and profoundly fascinating. He spent his time discussing the future of human labor with individuals who had made opposing automation central to their lives. Despite the heavy physical lifting required to transport his equipment alone, the portrait sittings provided deeply calming moments of genuine connection. He noted that traveling completely alone served as a potent reminder of the inherent value of authentic human interaction. Ultimately, his photographic series highlights the irreplaceable nature of human creativity in an increasingly mechanized world.
